ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'fried chicken' class (FOODON_03315595); no specific Jollibee Chickenjoy entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers fried chicken meat generically. No brand-specific Chickenjoy code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for branded fast-food fried chicken bucket product; left null.

serving.description: 6 pieces of fried chicken (3 original, 3 spicy)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is the full 6-piece bucket (approximately 900 g total), typically shared among 2–3 people, with each piece weighing approximately 150 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (NDB No. 05062)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Jollibee Chickenjoy is not individually listed in USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for batter-fried chicken (meat and skin), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for a breaded, deep-fried bone-in chicken product. The input record's per_100g values (energy 175 kcal, protein 12.5 g, fat 12.5 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g) are broadly consistent with USDA batter-fried chicken data and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 375 mg/100g is plausible for a seasoned fast-food product. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and full micronutrient profiles for this specific product are not available in USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jollibee Chickenjoy. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on analogous batter-fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and published literature. The breaded coating contributes refined carbohydrates (wheat flour, cornstarch) with a moderate GI, but the dominant macronutrient is protein and fat, which suppresses the overall glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 5.0 × 9 g carb per 100g × 900g serving / 100) / 100 ≈ (45 × 45) / 100 ≈ 20.25; however, per 100g carbohydrate is 5.0g, so GL per 100g = (45 × 5.0)/100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.0 per 100g reference. GL per full 900g serving = (45 × 45)/100 ≈ 20.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 375 mg per 100 g; a full 6-piece serving (900 g) delivers approximately 3,375 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit consumption of this product; if consumed, restrict to one piece and avoid all additional sodium sources (dipping sauces, sides) for the remainder of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5 g/100 g) limits acute glycemic impact, but high saturated fat (3.75 g/100 g) and deep-fried preparation impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume one to two pieces occasionally, removing the skin and breading to reduce saturated fat and refined carbohydrate load, and must avoid sugary dipping sauces that would raise the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of saturated fat (3.75 g/100 g), trans fat (0.13 g/100 g), cholesterol (62.5 mg/100 g), and high sodium (375 mg/100 g) presents a multi-factor cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this product; the trans fat content, even at 0.13 g/100 g, is clinically significant for LDL elevation, and the sodium load at full-serving quantities substantially raises blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; trans fat (0.13 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried products; if consumed, remove skin and breading to reduce AGE and trans fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jollibee's 6-Piece Chickenjoy Bucket (3 Original, 3 Spicy) is a popular fast-food fried chicken product from Jollibee, a Filipino quick-service restaurant chain with a significant presence in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The bucket contains six bone-in chicken pieces — three coated in the classic Original Chickenjoy seasoning blend and three in the Spicy Chickenjoy variant, which incorporates chili-based seasoning for added heat. Each piece is marinated, coated in a seasoned wheat flour and cornstarch batter, and deep-fried to produce a characteristically crispy, golden-brown skin encasing juicy, tender chicken meat. The Original variant delivers a mildly savory, umami-rich flavor, while the Spicy variant adds a moderate chili heat. Nutritionally, per 100 g the product provides approximately 175 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 12.5 g fat (of which 3.75 g is saturated), 5.0 g carbohydrate, and 375 mg sodium. The high sodium content (375 mg/100 g) makes a full 6-piece serving clinically significant for hypertension management, delivering approximately 3,375 mg sodium — well above the WHO daily recommended limit. The saturated fat and trace trans fat content (0.13 g/100 g) raise cardiovascular concern with regular consumption. The low carbohydrate content (5 g/100 g) results in a low glycemic index (estimated ~45) and low glycemic load per 100 g, making acute blood glucose impact modest, though the deep-fried preparation and saturated fat profile are unfavorable for long-term insulin sensitivity. The product is certified halal and is gluten-containing due to the wheat flour breading. It is commonly consumed as a shared meal or family-style dining occasion in Singapore and the Philippines.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Jollibee Chickenjoy?
Is Jollibee Chickenjoy high in fat?
Can I eat Chickenjoy if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Jollibee Chickenjoy is not directly listed in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy batter-fried chicken (meat and skin), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. The input record's values are broadly consistent with this reference and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy batter-fried chicken (meat and skin, NDB 05062) as no direct Jollibee Chickenjoy entry exists in any approved primary source. Input per_100g values are consistent with this reference and retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific branded fast-food product are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): deep-fried chicken with breading contains negligible vitamin C; USDA FoodData Central reports 0 mg vitamin C for batter-fried chicken. 4. trans_fat_g 0.13 g/100g is retained from input; this is plausible for a product deep-fried in partially hydrogenated or recycled frying oil. 5. Portions in the input record were inconsistent (multiple duplicate entries, conflicting gram weights for the same label); the serving size of 900 g for 6 pieces is used as the primary default. 6. Country of origin corrected from 'Singapore' (input error) to 'Philippines' — Jollibee is a Philippine-origin chain. 7. Primary cuisine corrected from 'Burgers' (input error) to 'Filipino Fast Food'. 8. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous batter-fried chicken from University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct measurement available. 9. Halal certification noted per input dietary_tags; this is consistent with Jollibee Singapore's halal-certified status.
